summary: Justin Brennan discusses the taxonomy of real estate asset classes, advocating for multifamily apartments (20-100+ units) and mobile homes as superior to single-family/condos due to economies of scale, cash flow, and tax incentives. While foundational philosophy aligns with Brian's target market (15-50 unit multifamily), the lesson focuses on asset-class selection rather than deal analysis or sourcing—Brian's stated drag areas.
takeaways: Avoid 'onesie twosies' (single-family/condos); consolidate into 20-50 unit or 100+ unit properties for economies of scale
Party begins at 5+ units; real game starts at 100+ units where true economies of scale emerge
Be 'inch wide and a mile deep'—specialize deeply in 1-3 asset classes rather than spreading thin across many
Multifamily apartments superior to office/commercial because tenants have fundamental need (food, water, shelter); office buildings go vacant in recessions/COVID
Current US rental mix ~30% renters vs 63% homeowners, trending toward European model with more renters over next 10 years
Mobile homes are phenomenal second-best asset class; both multifamily and mobile homes provide reliable cash flow, growth, tax incentives, and inflation hedges
Progress through asset classes: start single-family/condos, graduate to 2-4 units, then 5-20 units, then 20-50 units, then 100+ units

skill_candidates.change: Focus on markets with strong rental demand fundamentals (food/water/shelter necessity) and avoid overbuilt/recession-sensitive sectors like office space
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly rejects office buildings because tenants don't need physical space to operate (COVID example, Amazon/Microsoft in garages), advocates for apartments because 'people need a place to live' and rental % is growing toward European model

skill_candidates.change: Prioritize properties in the 20-50+ unit range to capture economies of scale rather than analyzing single small units across multiple locations
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin repeatedly emphasizes '50 people in one building, one location, one time' vs '50 single family homes all across different areas'—the consolidation creates operational efficiency that small deals cannot match

title: Lesson 1 - Types Of Real Estate
module: Module 6 - Let's Get Into The Details
